## Analyst Notes

### NOAA metric explanations (concise)
- **Planetary K index (Kp)** — Global 0–9 index of geomagnetic disturbance strength from ground magnetometers.. Why it matters: Higher Kp indicates stronger storms driven by solar-wind coupling (often with southward IMF Bz).. Physiology: Studies associate higher geomagnetic activity with small reductions in HRV (lower SDNN/RMSSD, HF) and higher cardiovascular risk in populations.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: negative (higher Kp → lower vagal/total HRV), with lags from 0 to ~72 h. (Refs: Vieira 2022; Gaisenok 2025; McCraty 2017; Papailiou 2023.).
- **Estimated Kp (nowcast)** — Near-real-time estimate of Kp prior to final processing.. Why it matters: Operational nowcast of geomagnetic variability.. Physiology: As for Kp.. Likely HRV: Negative correlation with vagal/total HRV is most probable. (Refs: As Kp.).
- **Boulder K index** — Local geomagnetic K index recorded at Boulder, Colorado (0–9 scale).. Why it matters: Highlights local disturbances that may differ from global Kp.. Physiology: Spatial variations of geomagnetic activity can modulate local HRV responses similarly to Kp.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: negative (higher local K → lower HRV). (Refs: Vieira 2022; Papailiou 2023.).
- **Dst (storm-time disturbance)** — Hourly nT index of ring current; more negative values indicate stronger geomagnetic storms.. Why it matters: Captures global storm intensity driven by magnetospheric currents.. Physiology: Storm periods linked to HRV decreases and cardiovascular events in population data.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: positive with |Dst| (more negative Dst → lower HRV). (Refs: Vieira 2022; Gaisenok 2025.).
- **Dst (7-day)** — Seven-day history/forecast of the Dst geomagnetic storm index.. Why it matters: Shows storm evolution and near-term expectations.. Physiology: Same implications as hourly Dst; track multi-day storm phases versus HRV recovery.. Likely HRV: More negative Dst intervals likely align with lower HRV. (Refs: Vieira 2022; Gaisenok 2025.).
- **Solar-wind speed** — Flow speed of the solar wind near Earth (km/s).. Why it matters: Higher speeds increase energy input to the magnetosphere and storm potential.. Physiology: Higher speed associated with increased heart rate and HRV changes consistent with stress responses.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: negative (higher speed → lower HRV) with short lags (0–72 h). (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018; Vencloviene 2020.).
- **Solar-wind proton density** — Particle number density (1/cm³).. Why it matters: Contributes to dynamic pressure; can precede sudden impulses and substorms.. Physiology: Less consistent alone; consider with speed/IMF and Kp/Dst.. Likely HRV: Possible negative HRV correlation when coincident with other drivers. (Refs: Vencloviene 2020 (pressure).).
- **IMF total field (Bt)** — Magnitude (nT) of the interplanetary magnetic field.. Why it matters: Stronger Bt with southward Bz increases coupling and storm strength.. Physiology: Context for Kp/Dst; not a direct HRV driver by itself.. Likely HRV: Indirect; effects mediated via geomagnetic activity indices. (Refs: Space weather coupling context.).
- **IMF Bz (GSE)** — North–south IMF component; Bz < 0 (southward) favors strong coupling.. Why it matters: Southward Bz is a key trigger for storms.. Physiology: Indirect driver; HRV effects via subsequent geomagnetic activity.. Likely HRV: Negative HRV correlation when Bz is sustained southward and followed by elevated Kp/Dst. (Refs: Space weather coupling context.).
- **F10.7 cm flux** — Solar 2.8 GHz radio flux (sfu), a proxy for EUV output and solar activity.. Why it matters: Higher F10.7 indicates more active Sun / ionospheric changes.. Physiology: Reports vary; some show small increases in HRV/parasympathetic indices with higher background activity.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation is plausible (context‑dependent). (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018.).
- **F10.7 flux (monthly)** — Monthly mean 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Background solar cycle driver for ionospheric conditions.. Physiology: Slow changes may track shifts in HRV baseline via solar-cycle modulation.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation plausible; interpret with long-term context. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018.).
- **F10.7 smoothed flux** — Smoothed monthly 10.7 cm solar flux highlighting solar-cycle trend.. Why it matters: Provides cleaner solar-cycle signal for long-term analysis.. Physiology: As above; use for trend-level analyses alongside HRV baselines.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation plausible. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018.).
- **GOES integral proton flux** — Particle flux above energy thresholds; used to define radiation storms (S‑scale).. Why it matters: Often accompanies CME‑driven storms impacting geomagnetic activity.. Physiology: Ground‑level HRV links are uncertain; treat as context with Kp/Dst and solar‑wind changes.. Likely HRV: Indirect; monitor alongside geomagnetic indices. (Refs: Operational/aviation context.).
- **GOES x‑ray flux** — Soft x‑ray irradiance (0.05–0.4/0.1–0.8 nm) used for flare classification.. Why it matters: Flares indicate active regions; not all cause storms without CME/IMF coupling.. Physiology: Direct HRV effects not established; use as solar context.. Likely HRV: Indirect via subsequent solar‑wind/geomagnetic changes. (Refs: Operational flare context.).
- **Predicted Kp (model)** — Short‑term model forecast of Kp at 1‑hour cadence.. Why it matters: Provides anticipation for geomagnetic variability.. Physiology: As for Kp; enables pre‑registration of analysis windows.. Likely HRV: Expected negative HRV correlation if predictions verify. (Refs: As Kp.).
- **F10.7 forecast (1 day)** — NOAA 1-day forecast of the 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Anticipates short-term solar EUV output changes.. Physiology: Use alongside Kp/Dst forecasts for expected HRV shifts.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation expected if values rise (contextual). (Refs: Operational forecast context.).
- **Fredericksburg a-index forecast (1 day)** — NOAA 1-day forecast of the Fredericksburg geomagnetic a-index.. Why it matters: Higher a-index reflects stronger geomagnetic agitation at mid-latitudes.. Physiology: Similar to Kp/Dst, elevated forecasts may precede HRV reductions if storms occur.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: negative (higher forecasted a-index → lower HRV). (Refs: Gaisenok 2025; Vencloviene 2020.).
- **Predicted sunspot number (monthly)** — NOAA prediction of monthly international sunspot number.. Why it matters: Tracks expected solar-cycle strength and background solar activity.. Physiology: Long-term shifts in solar activity may modulate HRV baselines via geomagnetic coupling.. Likely HRV: Slow positive HRV correlation plausible with higher sunspot forecasts. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018; McCraty 2017.).
- **Predicted 10.7 cm flux (monthly)** — Forecast of monthly 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Indicates expected EUV output for ionospheric state changes.. Physiology: Use for trend context alongside observed F10.7 and HRV baselines.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation plausible. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018.).
- **Sunspot number** — Observed monthly international sunspot number (dimensionless).. Why it matters: Proxy for solar magnetic activity and solar-cycle phase.. Physiology: Solar-cycle background may modulate baseline HRV over long periods (via F10.7, cosmic rays).. Likely HRV: Slow positive HRV correlation plausible with higher sunspot activity (contextual). (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018; McCraty 2017.).
- **F10.7 forecast (1 day)** — NOAA 1-day forecast of the 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Anticipates short-term solar EUV output changes.. Physiology: Use alongside Kp/Dst forecasts for expected HRV shifts.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation expected if values rise (contextual). (Refs: Operational forecast context.).
- **F10.7 forecast (1 day)** — NOAA 1-day forecast of the 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Anticipates short-term solar EUV output changes.. Physiology: Use alongside Kp/Dst forecasts for expected HRV shifts.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation expected if values rise (contextual). (Refs: Operational forecast context.).
- **Fredericksburg a-index forecast (1 day)** — NOAA 1-day forecast of the Fredericksburg geomagnetic a-index.. Why it matters: Higher a-index reflects stronger geomagnetic agitation at mid-latitudes.. Physiology: Similar to Kp/Dst, elevated forecasts may precede HRV reductions if storms occur.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: negative (higher forecasted a-index → lower HRV). (Refs: Gaisenok 2025; Vencloviene 2020.).
- **Fredericksburg a-index forecast (1 day)** — NOAA 1-day forecast of the Fredericksburg geomagnetic a-index.. Why it matters: Higher a-index reflects stronger geomagnetic agitation at mid-latitudes.. Physiology: Similar to Kp/Dst, elevated forecasts may precede HRV reductions if storms occur.. Likely HRV: Most probable HRV correlation: negative (higher forecasted a-index → lower HRV). (Refs: Gaisenok 2025; Vencloviene 2020.).
- **Predicted sunspot number (monthly)** — NOAA prediction of monthly international sunspot number.. Why it matters: Tracks expected solar-cycle strength and background solar activity.. Physiology: Long-term shifts in solar activity may modulate HRV baselines via geomagnetic coupling.. Likely HRV: Slow positive HRV correlation plausible with higher sunspot forecasts. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018; McCraty 2017.).
- **Predicted sunspot number (monthly)** — NOAA prediction of monthly international sunspot number.. Why it matters: Tracks expected solar-cycle strength and background solar activity.. Physiology: Long-term shifts in solar activity may modulate HRV baselines via geomagnetic coupling.. Likely HRV: Slow positive HRV correlation plausible with higher sunspot forecasts. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018; McCraty 2017.).
- **Predicted 10.7 cm flux (monthly)** — Forecast of monthly 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Indicates expected EUV output for ionospheric state changes.. Physiology: Use for trend context alongside observed F10.7 and HRV baselines.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation plausible. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018.).
- **Predicted 10.7 cm flux (monthly)** — Forecast of monthly 10.7 cm solar radio flux.. Why it matters: Indicates expected EUV output for ionospheric state changes.. Physiology: Use for trend context alongside observed F10.7 and HRV baselines.. Likely HRV: Small positive HRV correlation plausible. (Refs: Alabdulgader 2018.).
- **Solar probabilities (c class 1 day)** — NOAA probabilistic forecast (% chance) for flare classes or >10 MeV proton events over 1–3 days.. Why it matters: Higher probabilities indicate elevated risk of radiation storms and geomagnetic impacts.. Physiology: Anticipate potential HRV reductions if elevated probabilities lead to storms (via increased geomagnetic activity).. Likely HRV: Indirect: higher probabilities may precede periods of lower HRV should storms materialize. (Refs: Gaisenok 2025; operational NOAA guidance.).
